coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot-infused gel polymer electrolytes: A new horizon for redox-enhanced supercapacitors

Nazareth, Ronald Aquin

Desai, Nakul
coredata.dc:description: A new redox-based supercapacitor has been developed using carbon dots (CDs) derived from graphitic carbon nitride (gCN) and a biodegradable gel polymer electrolyte (GPE) composed of 2,3-hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as the polymer base, glycerol (Gly) as a plasticizer, and sodium perchlorate (NaClO₄) as the doping salt, with activated carbon (AC) and graphene serving as electrodes. A unique combination of CDs with GPE increases the internal conductivity and stability of the matrix, which is discussed in this paper for the first time. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis identified key functional groups that support polymerCDs interactions. X-ray diffraction (XRD) revealed a semicrystalline nature with enhanced amorphous characteristics due to the addition of CDs and salt, promoting ionic conductivity. Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) showed thermal stability, with degradation steps at 30–200°C (moisture loss) and 200–470°C (polymer decomposition), which are suitable for energy storage applications. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images revealed a small globular cluster-like surface morphology. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of the synthesized carbon dots revealed less than 10 nm sizes. The fabricated supercapacitor exhibited a maximum specific capacitance of 52 F/g for the AC electrode and 15 F/g for the graphene electrode, as determined via cyclic voltammetry (CV). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis revealed that the AC electrode had less resistance than the graphene electrode. This means that the AC electrode had better ionic conductivity and electrode synergy. GCD analysis at a current density of 1 mA/g revealed that the graphene electrode achieved 11 F/g capacitance, 3.8 Wh/kg energy density, and 0.318 kW/kg power density. In contrast, the AC electrode exhibited 8 F/g, 2.8 Wh/kg, and 0.315 kW/kg, confirming redox contributions at the electrode/electrolyte interface.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new redox-based supercapacitor has been developed using carbon dots (CDs) derived from graphitic carbon nitride (gCN) and a biodegradable gel polymer electrolyte (GPE) composed of 2,3-hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as the polymer base, glycerol (Gly) as a plasticizer, and sodium perchlorate (NaClO₄) as the doping salt, with activated carbon (AC) and graphene serving as electrodes. A unique combination of CDs with GPE increases the internal conductivity and stability of the matrix, which is discussed in this paper for the first time. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis identified key functional groups that support polymerCDs interactions. X-ray diffraction (XRD) revealed a semicrystalline nature with enhanced amorphous characteristics due to the addition of CDs and salt, promoting ionic conductivity. Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) showed thermal stability, with degradation steps at 30–200°C (moisture loss) and 200–470°C (polymer decomposition), which are suitable for energy storage applications. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images revealed a small globular cluster-like surface morphology. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of the synthesized carbon dots revealed less than 10 nm sizes. The fabricated supercapacitor exhibited a maximum specific capacitance of 52 F/g for the AC electrode and 15 F/g for the graphene electrode, as determined via cyclic voltammetry (CV). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis revealed that the AC electrode had less resistance than the graphene electrode. This means that the AC electrode had better ionic conductivity and electrode synergy. GCD analysis at a current density of 1 mA/g revealed that the graphene electrode achieved 11 F/g capacitance, 3.8 Wh/kg energy density, and 0.318 kW/kg power density. In contrast, the AC electrode exhibited 8 F/g, 2.8 Wh/kg, and 0.315 kW/kg, confirming redox contributions at the electrode/electrolyte interface.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy storage is pivotal in advancing a resilient and sustainable energy infrastructure [1,2]. By enabling the efficient capture and distribution of renewable energy, it reduces reliance on fossil fuels. This technology is integral in shaping the future of energy systems, ensuring greater reliability, efficiency, and environmental sustainability [3,4]. Supercapacitors are widely recognized as promising energy storage technologies for the future. They stand out among various energy storage devices because of their exceptional cyclic stability and high power density [5–9]. Moreover, supercapacitors offer advantages over batteries, including a longer lifespan, enhanced safety, and lower manufacturing costs [10,11].

Electrolytes are crucial components of electrochemical energy storage devices, facilitating internal charge transfer through ionic movement between oppositely charged electrodes. Additionally, they infiltrate the porous structure of high-surface-area electrodes, such as activated carbon, optimizing charge exchange at the electrodeelectrolyte interfaces for enhanced performance. In recent years, gel polymer electrolytes (GPEs) have gained significant attention as promising alternatives for flexible supercapacitors, offering higher ionic conductivity and enhanced mechanical properties than liquid electrolytes do [12]. GPEs consist of a solid polymer framework that encapsulates liquid electrolytes. Their unique composition enables them to support the flexible architecture of supercapacitors while ensuring structural stability, preventing leakage, and serving multiple roles as electrolytes, separators, and even binders [12,13]. Commonly used polymer matrices for gel polymer electrolytes (GPEs) include poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA), poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF), poly(vinylidene fluoride-hexafluoropropylene) (PVDF-HFP), polyacrylonitrile (PAN), and polyethylene oxide (PEO) [14–20]. Nevertheless, synthetic polymers often used as host polymers have disadvantages, including high cost and lack of ecological sustainability. Hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC) is an optimal choice since it is soluble in nontoxic solvents, displays excellent film-forming capacity, and has great mechanical flexibility. Owing to its range of properties, HEC is broadly used in biomedicine, agriculture, cosmetics, membranes, food, etc. [21,22]. Brige et al. [23] explored the potential of HEC-based solid electrolytes to increase the reversibility of Zn batteries. Similarly, Azha et al. [24] investigated an NH4Br-doped HEC-dextran composite, facilitating ionic conduction and electrode separation in energy storage systems. In another study, Chavan et al. [25] reported distinct ionic conduction behavior in a NaPF6-infused HEC, demonstrating its suitability as an electrolyte separator in sodium-ion batteries. HEC, when combined with other polymers and enriched with fillers, salts, or acids, is a practical component in gel-based electrolytes for capacitors and supercapacitors [26].

Carbon dots (CDs), a new class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with sizes smaller than 10 nm, have garnered significant attention since their initial discovery during the separation and purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004 [27]. Carbon dots (CDs) are quasispherical nanostructures composed of carbon atoms with both sp² and sp³ hybridization, featuring hydrophilic functional groups on their surfaces [28]. Structurally, CDs consist primarily of amorphous carbon interspersed with nanocrystalline regions of sp²-hybridized graphitic domains, contributing to their spherical morphology [29,30]. One of the key attributes of CDs is their surface functionalization capability, which allows for incorporating diverse chemical groups to increase their stability and improve their functional properties. This adaptability makes CDs highly versatile, as they can be integrated with various materials, including polymers, metals, metal oxides, and nanoparticles, to develop advanced hybrid systems. These composites leverage the synergistic interactions between CDs and their host materials, leading to enhanced performance and an expanded range of applications across multiple fields [31–33]. Graphitic carbon nitrides (g-C₃N₄) have attracted significant attention in the quest for efficient and long-lasting energy storage systems because of their environmentally friendly nature, cost-effectiveness, high in-plane nitrogen content, and remarkable chemical and thermal stability [34,35]. Despite their promising properties, research on g-C₃N₄ in electrochemical energy storage systems is still in its early stages compared with other electrochemically active materials [36,37]. In rechargeable batteries, the lone pairs of electrons in pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen sites are widely recognized for their crucial role in metal ion accumulation and enhancing surface capacitive behavior [38,39]. Given its high pyridinic nitrogen content, g-C₃N₄ is regarded as an auspicious material for energy storage applications, offering the potential to improve electrochemical performance significantly.

Plasticization is a widely adopted approach to increase electrolyte conductivity. Various plasticizers, including sorbitol, glycerol, and polyethylene glycol, have been explored for this purpose [40]. Among them, glycerol stands out due to its extensive usage, which can be attributed to its low molecular weight, high polarity, and remarkable dielectric constant. These properties make it highly compatible with biopolymer electrolyte systems. Additionally, glycerol is crucial in improving electrodeelectrolyte interfacial interactions, a key factor in optimizing energy storage device performance [41]. Incorporating various salts into host polymer matrices enhances their ionic conductivity while increasing the amorphous nature of the polymer electrolyte, facilitating ion transport. Unlike lithium salts, sodium-based alternatives are gaining prominence in energy storage because of their significantly lower cost (30–40 times lower) and abundant availability [42]. Among these, sodium perchlorate stands out for its stability at room temperature and resistance to moderate heating without decomposition. This stability is particularly beneficial for the electrolytes required to function under diverse conditions. Additionally, sodium perchlorate has high water solubility, making it well-suited for preparing aqueous electrolyte solutions [43,44].

In this study, eco-friendly CDs were synthesized from graphitic carbon nitride (gCN) and incorporated into a biobased GPE to develop a sodium-based supercapacitor. The CDs were embedded in a gel polymer matrix composed of 2,3-hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC), glycerol (Gly) as a plasticizer, and sodium perchlorate (NaClO₄) as the doping salt. This novel approach leverages CDs derived from environmentally friendly precursors to increase the conductivity and stability of the GPE matrix. By integrating green chemistry principles, this work paves the way for developing high-performance, sustainable, and eco-friendly energy storage technology.

50 g of urea from Loba Chemie was placed in a semiclosed ceramic crucible and heated in a muffle furnace at 500°C for two hours, with a slow heating rate of 3–4°C per minute. This process yielded approximately 3 g of off-whitish gCN. To obtain CDs, 0.1 g of gCN was sonicated in a mixed solution of concentrated sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄, 20 ml) and nitric acid (HNO₃, 60 ml) for 16 hours. The resulting mixture was washed with 400 ml of deionized water to remove impurities and then centrifuged. The synthesis process is schematically represented in Fig. 1.

HEC (Mw = 250,000) was purchased from SigmaAldrich, and the stock solution was prepared by dissolving 2 g of HEC in 100 millilitres of distilled water. The mixture was placed on a magnetic stirrer for half an hour. As a result, a uniform HEC stock solution was obtained.
GPEs were prepared with HEC and PVA in a 9:1 ratio, 1 ml of CD solution, 3–4 drops of Gly (Merck), and 0.01–0.03 g NaClO4 (Loba Chemie) with various sample combinations, as shown in Table 1.

The prepared combinations were placed separately in clean 10 ml beakers and allowed to undergo initial gelation at room temperature. The samples were subsequently placed in an oven at 40°C for 48 hours to facilitate the formation of gel polymer electrolytes (GPEs) before further studies were performed.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) measurements were conducted at room temperature using a PerkinElmer Spectrum Two FTIR spectrometer. The spectra were recorded between 4000 and 400 cm⁻¹ with a resolution of 4 cm⁻¹ . Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) measurements of the GPEs were conducted using a PerkinElmer TGA 4000 instrument. The measurements were performed under a nitrogen atmosphere with a flow rate of 50 ml min⁻¹ over a temperature range from 30°C to 700°C at a heating rate of 10°C min⁻¹ . Data were recorded from the initial heating cycle. X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies were performed via a Rigaku MiniFlex XRD instrument, and the diffraction patterns were recorded for 2θ values between 0° and 90°. The samples of GPEs were cut into small cubes, followed by freeze-drying in a deep freezer, subsequent storage in a nitrogen atmosphere, and exposure to high vacuum conditions. Microscopy images were captured via a scanning electron microscope (SEM), specifically by ZEISS EVO 18 special edition (S1, S2, S5) and APREO 2S Thermo Fisher model (S3, S4). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were acquired via an FEI Tecnai G2–30 transmission electron microscope. Electrochemical characterization, including cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and galvanostatic chargedischarge (GCD) studies, was conducted via a BioLogic SP-50e instrument. The specific capacitance (C, F/g) of the supercapacitor was calculated from the CV curve via the following equation:

The specific capacitance (C, F/g) of the supercapacitor was determined from chargedischarge curves via the following equation [45]:

The energy density (E, Wh/kg) and power density (P, kW/kg) of the supercapacitor were obtained from the following equations [45,46]:

where A is the area under the CV curve, k is the scan rate (mV/s), m is the mass of the active material (g), ΔV represents the potential window (V), I is the discharge current (A), and Δt is the discharge time (s).

The electrode material for supercapacitor fabrication was prepared from activated carbon (AC) derived from areca fibers [47] and graphene (Merck), with polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) binder dissolved in N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) at a 3:1 ratio. The PVDF binder solution was mixed with AC at a weight ratio of 0.3:1 and ground using a mortar and pestle to form a slurry. This slurry was coated onto two stainless steel electrodes. The supercapacitor cell was assembled by sandwiching a gel polymer electrolyte (GPE) between two prepared AC and graphene electrodes. The unit cells were then sealed in plastic with two wires extending outside. Electrochemical characterization was performed via cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and galvanostatic chargedischarge (GCD) studies, which were conducted via a BioLogic SP-50e instrument.

Fig. 2 shows the FTIR spectra of pure gCN, gCN-CDs, and samples S1-S5. The peak at 807 cm−1 is due to the tri-s-heptazine or tri-s-triazine unit of gCN, whose intensity disappears with increasing sonication time, indicating damage to the basic unit [48]. The stretching vibration of the repeating unit of heptazine causes peaks at 1641, 1570, 1462, and 1412 cm−1, according to the FTIR study of gCN. The C-N/C-N stretching vibration mode corresponds to the strong band between 1200 and 1650 cm−1. As the sonication period was extended, the peak intensity in this region decreased, suggesting that sonication damages the CN bond. With increasing ultrasonication time, the broad peaks observed between 3000 and 3500 cm⁻¹ corresponding to the stretching vibrations of NH bonds gradually lose intensity and even shift to higher energy levels [49]. The formation of hydrogen bonds between water and carbon dots in the region at 3300 cm-1 (-OH stretching) indicates the solvation of the carbon dots. S1 shows strong and broad bands at 3334 cm⁻¹ and 1042 cm⁻¹ , corresponding to the hydroxyl group in the pyranose unit and the C-O-C skeletal vibration [50,51], respectively. The band at 2886 cm⁻¹ is associated with C-H stretching due to the presence of CH and CH₂ groups [52]. The absorption peak at 1643 cm⁻¹ is attributed to the bending mode of naturally absorbed water [50,51], whereas the peak at 1357 cm⁻¹ is linked to O-H bending [50]. The area corresponding to the O–H stretching vibration region at 3294 cm⁻¹ is greater for glycerol. This indicates the hydrophilic behavior of glycerol, with the increased area attributed to hydrogen bonds formed by the hydroxyl groups in both the HEC and glycerol structures. For the S2 spectra, a new peak emerges at 1200 cm⁻¹ due to CN stretching in the CDs, whereas the prominent peaks shift due to the strong interactions between the HEC and CDs. The Na salt-doped GPEs show peaks at 3400 cm⁻¹ , 1650 cm⁻¹ , and 1100--1000 cm⁻¹ in S3, S4, and S5, respectively, enhancing the interaction between the polymer chain, CDs, and salt, leading to more pronounced absorption features.

XRD measurements were performed on the gel polymer electrolyte to study its crystalline/amorphous nature. Fig. 3 shows the XRD patterns of S1, S2, S3, S4, and S5. The diffraction pattern of S1 exhibited diffraction peaks at 2θ = 23.2°, 31°, and 48.5°. The strong and sharp peak at 2θ = 23.2° depicts the semicrystalline nature of HEC [53]. In S2, the peak located at 2θ∼27.6° corresponds to the (002) plane arising from the stacked graphite-like layers, which are held together by weak van der Waals forces between porous gCN layers [54]. The intensity of the peaks decreased at 27.6° for S3, S4, and S5 with the addition of salt to the polymer, which reveals that the carbon dots and doped salt enhance the amorphous nature of the electrolytes. Similar to the Hodge et al. [55] criterion, the XRD results confirmed the correlation between the intensity of the peak and the degree of crystallinity.

Fig. 4 shows that degradation occurs in two distinct steps [56,57]. Initially, all samples exhibited minor mass loss (5–25 %) between 30°C and 200°C, which was attributed to moisture loss and glycerol decomposition [58]. The second significant decomposition step occurs between approximately 200°C and 470°C and is attributed to the decomposition of the COO− group within the polymer system [57]. S1 shows the lowest stability, starting to decompose at ∼200°C (major loss: 200–400°C); S2 follows at ∼220°C (major loss: 220–420°C), showing moderate stability; S3 and S4 decompose at ∼230°C (major loss: 230–450°C), reflecting better stability; S5 is the most stable, begins decomposing at ∼250°C (major loss: 250–470°C), and retains ∼10–15 % residual weight at higher temperatures. The addition of carbon dots (CDs) and salt gradually increased the thermal stability of the GPEs.

Fig. 5 shows SEM images of the GPEs. S1 shows a wrinkled surface, indicating that the solvent of the gel was able to form pools between the polymer matrix during SEM analysis under high vacuum conditions. These pools were quickly dried, leaving behind the wrinkles. As shown in Fig. 5 (S2), after the addition of CDs, the surface of the GPE is smoother and more uniform than that of S1. The gCN-CDs act as conductive fillers for electrochemical reactions by forming conductive pathways throughout the polymer matrix, promoting efficient electron transfer, and enhancing the redox behavior of the system. As a result, the wrinkled surface is avoided. The prominent white flower-like feature observed in S2 is likely due to the clustering of the polymer during peeling from the beaker. As shown in Fig. 5 (S3), the concentration of NaClO4 was the lowest, which had little influence on the interaction with the polymer chain; hence, a smooth surface was observed; however, slightly wrinkled nature appeared. With the further addition of NaClO4 (S4 and S5), the formation of small globular clusters on the surface is evident, suggesting that the salt disrupts intermolecular bonds within the gel matrix and strongly interacts with the gel matrix. This disruption increases the surface area and creates additional active sites on the gel. The rough, globular texture, along with the embedded CDs, facilitates better ion diffusion and charge transfer, leading to a higher energy density, improved capacitance, and faster charge-discharge cycles.

Fig. 6 shows the HRTEM and SAED images of the gCN-CDs. Fig. 6a shows a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of the gCN-CDs, revealing a crystalline structure. The scale bar suggests that the observed structures are on the nanometer scale, emphasizing the fine structural details of the gCN-CDs. In Fig. 6b, the SAED pattern of the gCN-CDs shows a concentric ring structure, indicating the polycrystalline nature of the composite material [59]. The gCN-CDs had an interplanar d-spacing of 0.32 nm, corresponding to the (002) planes, which signifies the interlayer distance between the graphitic planes within the structure and is consistent with the XRD data. The crystalline structure and nanoscale features of gCN-CDs provide a high surface area and efficient pathways for ion and electron transport. These properties enhance redox activity and electrochemical performance, making gCN-CDs highly suitable for improving the energy storage capacity and efficiency of supercapacitors.

Figs. 7 and 8 show the CV curves of S2 and S5 with graphene and AC electrodes at scan rates ranging from 5 to 100 mV/s. The graphene electrode achieved a maximum specific capacitance of 15 F/g for S5 at 5 mV/s, whereas the AC electrode reached 52 F/g for S5 at the same scan rate. The reversible Faradaic redox features reveal two distinct asymmetrical peaks, indicating that the electrochemical activity of gCN is linked to pseudocapacitance. The redox peaks in the CV profile highlight the interaction between the electrodes and electrolytes [60]. The AC electrode outperforms the graphene electrode in capacitance and electrochemical performance because of its larger surface area and high porosity. Additionally, the redox peaks suggest reversible electron transfer at the electrodeelectrolyte interface. Higher scan rates increase the current, indicating faster ion diffusion and charge transfer. The nonrectangular CV shape combines double-layer and pseudocapacitive charge storage [61].

Fig. 9 shows the EIS plots for S2–S5 for (a) graphene and (b) AC electrodes in the frequency range from 1 MHz to 100 mHz. The high frequency region is shown in the magnified plots in the insets of Fig. 9a and b. The Nyquist plots obtained for graphene and AC electrodes exhibited ideal electrochemical capacitance behavior, characterized by a nearly linear imaginary part of the Warburg impedance at low frequencies [62]. This may be attributed to the low Faradaic resistance within the electrolyte due to capacitive behavior. Fig. 9a shows that the Rct values were 14.1, 13.8, 13.2, and 13.1 Ω for S2, S3, S4, and S5, respectively. These findings indicate that the addition of salts increased the ionic conductivity. In Fig. 9b, the Rct values were 20.2, 12.4, 12.1, and 11.8 Ω for S2, S3, S4, and S5, respectively. This finding indicates that S5 again has the most pronounced decrease in impedance, with a notable downward shift compared with the other samples, whereas S2 has the highest impedance values. However, the AC electrode-based EIS plots showed less resistance than the graphene-based electrodes. Implying better synergy between activated carbon and the CD-infused GPEs. The progressive divergence in curve slopes and intercepts highlights significant differences in resistance and capacitive behavior among the samples.

Fig. 10 displays the GCD curves of S5 supercapacitors with (a) graphene and (b) AC electrodes at various current densities (0.1–1 mA/g) within a potential window of 1 V. The nonideal shapes of these curves indicate Faradaic contributions to the charge accumulation process, which is characteristic of a pseudocapacitor [63]. These results align with the CV test data. For the graphene electrode at 1 mA/g current density, a specific capacitance (C) of 11 F/g, an energy density of 3.8 Wh/kg, and a power density of 0.318 kW/kg were obtained. For the AC electrode at 1 mA/g current density, a specific capacitance (C) of 8 F/g, an energy density of 2.8 Wh/kg, and a power density of 0.315 kW/kg were obtained, indicating redox reactions at the electrode/electrolyte interface. A comparative table consisting of specific capacitance, energy density, and power density values of other reported literature is shown in Table 2.

A possible mechanism inside the GPE is depicted in Fig. 11. The composite system consists of HEC and PVA segments, which form the polymer network. Gly acts as a plasticizer by reducing intermolecular forces within the polymer matrix, enhancing flexibility, and facilitating efficient ion and electron transport for improved electrochemical performance. gCN-CDs are uniformly dispersed within the polymer matrix, as shown in the HRTEM image. gCN-CDs act as active sites for electrochemical reactions by forming conductive pathways throughout the network, promoting efficient electron transfer, which enhances the redox behavior of the system. This suggests good synergy between the CDs and gCN. Integrating sodium ions enhanced the current response/high area under the CV curve.

Based on the characterization studies, the most likely mechanism of charge transport in the fabricated supercapacitor through the GPEs and AC/graphene electrode is explained in Fig. 12. FTIR, XRD, and TGA characterization studies revealed strong interactions between CDs, HEC, Gly, and salt within the gel system. SEM studies indicated that the Na-doped CD-based GPEs exhibited a rounded texture, enhancing ion diffusion and the charge transfer mechanism. The redox peaks observed in the CV studies are attributed by introducing gCN-CDs into the HEC polymer matrix, facilitating redox processes at the electrolyte/electrode interface and thus contributing to pseudocapacitance. Compared with graphene electrodes, AC electrodes have greater capacitance and superior electrochemical performance because of their high surface area and porosity. The GCD results are also consistent with the data obtained from the CV tests. The EIS results indicated capacitance behavior with lower resistance, suggesting that the fabricated supercapacitor exhibited efficient charge storage capabilities and enhanced conductivity. Gel polymer electrolytes can also be explored by integrating carbon-based composites and bioinspired materials to enhance their electrochemical performance, sensitivity, stability, and multifunctional capabilities, potentially improving energy storage, detection, and electromagnetic shielding [76–79].

A novel redox-mediated supercapacitor has been successfully fabricated using carbon dots (CDs) derived from graphitic carbon nitride (gCN) and a biodegradable gel polymer electrolyte (GPE) composed of 2,3-hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as the polymer matrix, glycerol (Gly) as a plasticizer, and sodium perchlorate (NaClO₄) as the doping salt, with activated carbon (AC) and graphene as electrodes. The fabricated supercapacitor exhibited specific capacitances of 15 F/g and 52 F/g for the graphene and activated carbon electrodes, respectively, as determined by cyclic voltammetry. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) revealed capacitive behavior with a lower resistance for the AC electrode. From a GCD at a current density of 1 mA/g, the graphene electrode showed a specific capacitance of 11 F/g, an energy density of 3.8 Wh/kg, and a power density of 0.318 kW/kg, whereas the activated carbon electrode achieved a specific capacitance of 8 F/g, an energy density of 2.8 Wh/kg, and a power density of 0.315 kW/kg. These results underscore the significant role of carbon dots in enhancing energy storage performance and highlight their potential for further applications in energy storage and conversion technologies.
